title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Blockade fails to intimidate dipnetters Published: June 12th, 2005 Last Modified: June 12th, 2005 at 12:48 AM WOOD CANYON -- The winds that so often blow a pall of glacier dust up the Copper River were silent as the morning sun kissed the east-facing cliffs near Spirit Mountain. Sitting nestled in a river-carved seat in the rock, with the handle of a dipnet braced between two convenient outcrops, it was tempting to doze in the sunshine. But there was no chance of that. The salmon wouldn't allow it. Unable to see it beneath the surface of the turbulent flow of glacial meltwater, they blindly thumped into the net. The handle shivered with their energy when they hit the back of the bag. The reflexive jerk that followed would remove the fish from the marine world forever. One sec…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Use a little angling imagination FISHING REPORT: Define your passions, research the possibilities. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: June 25th, 2004 Last Modified: May 30th, 2005 at 05:17 AM The fishing Tuesday was exquisite -- the number of fish, their relative size and the variety of species caught was comparable to what you'd expect from a remote Southwest Alaska river. There were rainbow trout, Dolly Varden and some of the biggest grayling I've seen this side of Ugashik Narrows. All lurked among the scarlet forms of pre-spawn king salmon, which the smaller fish followed much as wolves shadow migrating caribou herds. I spent the day working my way upriver, wading and casting where I could, sometimes busting through boreal jungle to reach the next gravel bar. Each pool held it…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Climber goes to the top with a clear head MANAGING RISK: Ed Viesturs took his time to reach his life's quest. By JOE ROBINSON Los Angeles Times Published: June 19th, 2005 Last Modified: June 19th, 2005 at 04:54 AM The lung-busting is finally over. Ed Viesturs' long-running quest to become the first American to climb all 14 of the world's 8,000-meter peaks -- 26,240 feet and higher -- ended in triumph last month when he topped his arch nemesis, avalanche-belching Annapurna. It was a last act fit for a drama primer. Viesturs, who has topped Everest six times, was inspired to take up the climbing life by Maurice Herzog's "Annapurna," the heroic tale of the first ascent of the mountain, which was also the first 8,000-meter peak ever climbed. Though Herzog lost most of his fingers and toes from…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Unexplored small stream yields big fish RAINBOWS: Anglers delight in a late-season discovery. By KEN MARSH Anchorage Daily News Published: September 12th, 2003 Last Modified: May 30th, 2005 at 05:35 AM The strike was vicious, shocking as a thunderbolt. One moment I was drifting a salmon-egg fly pattern through a deep run, and the next I was gripping my 8-weight rod with both hands as an enormous fish peeled off with incredible force. Standing on a sandbar nearby, my friend Pat Krepel of Delta Junction saw the torpedolike wake as the fish tore downstream. "Salmon!" he hollered. "No," I called back. "Trout!" The water had boiled when the fish struck, and I'd seen a bright salmon-sized flash as it took off. But this fish was much stronger than the dark, spawning silver salmon still found scat…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Dozer a good bet for Valley rail trail Published: June 5th, 2005 Last Modified: June 5th, 2005 at 05:20 AM Click to enlarge PALMER-MOOSE CREEK RAILROAD TRAIL -- Seen through the root wad of a cottonwood tree hanging off the undercut bank that this trail runs over, the raging waters of the Matanuska River tell part of the story about the troubles plaguing the route for the state's first Rails-to-Trails project. All over America, Rails-to-Trails has been taking abandoned railroad right-of-ways and turning them into great new routes for biking and hiking. The long-deserted Sutton arm of the Alaska Railroad -- the rails now gone but the rail bed significantly intact -- was to have joined those pathways. But nature seems to have other ideas. The corridors that hold Alaska's big, p…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing report: Sportfish biologists are finding where the trout roam KEN MARSH FISHING REPORT Published: June 9th, 2005 Last Modified: June 9th, 2005 at 04:04 AM Wonder where the rainbow trout are holding on your favorite Parks Highway trout stream? You might be surprised, according to Richard Yanusz. What's the hot fly pattern for catching those trout -- some as long as your arm? He's got a handle on that too. Yanusz didn't exactly say so Monday at a gathering of the Alaska Flyfishers Club, but there's no denying he has one of the best jobs in Alaska. A sportfish biologist with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, he leads the state's Susitna River Wild Rainbow Trout Research Project. His duties take him to remote stretches of Willow, Montana and Clear creeks, among other Parks Highwa…
